heading: NOTE AL SAGGIO INTRODUTTIVO CLXI
card_summary: This chunk consists entirely of the scholarly endnotes (notes 485–579) to Campanini's introductory essay on Zorzi's De Harmonia Mundi. The notes document the kabbalistic sources Zorzi used, tracing texts by Reuchlin, Avraham ben David, Shemu'el Ibn Motot, Joseph Gikatilla, Abraham Abulafia, Moses Botarel, and the Sefer Yetzirah tradition. Campanini identifies bibliographic phantoms (including one note on the "Colloquium Heptaplomeres"), corrects misidentifications in the French translation of De Harmonia Mundi, and tracks Zorzi's reception from John Donne's satirical catalogue to Agrippa von Nettesheim. A dense series of scriptural cross-references (Psalms, Job, Matthew, Revelation, Isaiah, Lamentations) drawn directly from De Harmonia Mundi are keyed to their biblical sources.
essay:
## Overview

Chunk 37 (pp. 161–166) is a continuous block of critical endnotes (nos. 485–579) appended to Campanini's introductory essay. These notes are entirely apparatus rather than primary Zorzi text: they identify Zorzi's kabbalistic sources, correct errors in the transmission of De Harmonia Mundi, track the book's reception history, and supply an extensive series of biblical citations keyed to passages in the main text.

## Kabbalistic Source Identification

### Reuchlin and Avraham ben David (notes 485–486)

Note 485 addresses a citation in Johann Reuchlin's Kabbalistic writing. Campanini observes that Reuchlin's single quotation appears to derive not from the text he names but from the introduction to the commentary on the Sefer Yetzirah traditionally attributed to Avraham ben David (RABaD). The exact words Reuchlin uses are quoted:

> "Et liber etiam quem edidit Rabi Tedacus Levi de decem numerationibus cabalisticis"

<cite>Note 485, p. 161</cite>

Campanini refers to Scholem's work (SCHOLEM 1927, pp. 176–177) and the BUSI–CAMPANINI 1995 edition. Note 486 cites ZUNZ 1845, p. 432 for further context on this attribution problem.

### The Halberstam Manuscript and Scholem's Discovery (note 487)

Note 487 is a substantial reference explaining that Scholem first announced his discovery of the relevant manuscript source on the occasion of receiving the Reuchlin Prize from the city of Pforzheim. Campanini cites the published record of that address (Pforzheimer Reuchlinpreis 1955–1995, Heidelberg 1996, pp. 119–131). He also notes a letter from Scholem to Joseph Blau (author of BLAU 1944) on the same matter (SCHOLEM 1994, pp. 300–301). Moshe Idel's dissent from the identification of the Halberstam 444 manuscript as Reuchlin's source is noted via GOODMAN 1993.

### Bibliographic Phantoms (notes 488–489)

Note 488 flags a "fantasma bibliografico" — a bibliographic ghost — whose creation and later career, including traces in the Colloquium Heptaplomeres of Jean Bodin, are treated in detail in CAMPANINI 2009a. This is a significant scholarly point: a spurious or misidentified kabbalistic title circulated through Christian Kabbalist literature, including Zorzi's own network.

## Moses Botarel and the Sefer Yetzirah Commentary Tradition (notes 490–491, 511, 513–514, 530)

Multiple notes track Zorzi's use of the Commentary on the Sefer Yetzirah attributed to Moses Botarel. Note 490 cites Botarel's Commentary at p. 31. Note 491 cross-references De Harmonia Mundi 2,1,4 and connects it to the first chapter of Shemu'el Ibn Motot's Meshovev netivot (with references to SANDMAN 2006 for both English translation and Hebrew text). Notes 513–514 continue this line, identifying Zorzi's unnamed source on the ten Sefiroth with Botarel's Commentary (pp. 38–39), while noting a possible alternative — Abraham De Balmes's Iggeret ha-'asiriyah (Epistle of the Decade, 1509), though this is judged less likely since Zorzi treated his source as anonymous.

## Joseph Gikatilla and Abraham Abulafia (notes 500, 531)

Note 500 identifies a passage Zorzi uses from Joseph Gikatilla's Sha'are tzedeq (Gates of Righteousness), at the Cracow 1881 edition, p. 17a, noting that François Secret had already flagged this passage (SECRET 1973, p. 199).

Note 531 is particularly detailed. It quotes directly from De Harmonia Mundi 2,7,2:

> "David enim sub eius persona loquens in psalmo secundo ait: Dominus dixit ad me: Filius meus es tu, ego hodie genui te, ubi in Hebraeo habetur הַיּוֹם יְלִדְתִּיךָ [Sal. 2,7], haiom ielidtica: per quod vocabulum intelligitur aeternitas sine aliquo determinato tempore, ut bene interpretatur author libri qui dicitur Eloquia pulchritudinis"

<cite>Note 531, p. 163; quoting De Harmonia Mundi 2,7,2</cite>

Campanini notes a correction: this passage does not actually appear in the Imre Shefer (Words of Beauty) attributed to Abraham Abulafia, as Zorzi implies, but rather in Abulafia's Chayye ha-'olam ha-ba (Life of the World to Come). He cites GROS 1999a, p. 129 for the Hebrew text and IDEL 1996, pp. 73 and n. 30 for an Abulafian commentary on the same verse. This is a significant editorial correction to a Christological exegesis: Zorzi interprets the Hebrew word *haiom* ("today") in Psalm 2:7 as signifying eternity rather than a specific time, a reading he attributes to a kabbalistic authority which Campanini now precisely identifies.

## The French Translation Error (note 521)

Note 521 registers an important translator's error in the 1578 French version of De Harmonia Mundi (L'harmonie du monde, Paris 1578):

> il traduttore francese del De harmonia mundi ha frainteso il senso del titolo indicato da Zorzi, rendendolo "le livre de ce qu'on doibt croire" (p. 517), come se l'ebraico suonasse "sefer ha-emunot"

<cite>Note 521, p. 162</cite>

The French translator misread Zorzi's Hebrew title as if it meant "the book of what one ought to believe" (Sefer ha-Emunot), when the correct reading is different. Secret (1985) noted the passage without commentary. This demonstrates Campanini's attentiveness to the reception and distortion of Zorzi's kabbalistic references across the European tradition.

## Daniel Bomberg, Felix Pratensis, and the Promise of Christian Kabbalah (note 523)

Note 523 quotes Daniel Bomberg's programmatic statement about publishing kabbalistic texts, from CAMPANINI 1997b:

> "Polliceor quoque maiora, atque altiora praeceptore meo omnium doctissimo fratre Felice Pratensi authore me propediem editurum, si quid tamen otii nactus fuero, ac si demum licitum fuerit, Cabalistices profundissimae Theologiae libros homine quidem christiano dignos me aliquot exaraturum, ex quibus quantum suavitatis hausturus sis, si te hisce profundius immerseris, non facile dixerim, doctiorum, ac consummatiorum id sane iudicum fuerit"

<cite>Note 523, p. 163</cite>

This promise — by the famous Venice printer and his teacher Felix of Prato — to publish kabbalistic theology worthy of a Christian man situates Zorzi within the broader Venetian milieu of Hebrew printing and Christian Kabbalist ambition.

## Zorzi's Reception: John Donne and the Satirical Catalogue (note 550)

Note 550 documents Zorzi's presence in John Donne's work. Donne cites Zorzi explicitly in the Essays in Divinity and the Biothanatos (though the name is misspelled "Zorgi" in SIMPSON 1952). More strikingly, in the posthumous Catalogus Librorum Aulicorum (The Courtier's Library, 1650), Donne includes a parodic title attributed to "Francisco Georgio Veneto":

> Librum Tobiae esse canonicum. Ubi ex Rabbinis et secretioribus Theologis numerantur pili caudae ejus canis, ex quorum varia retortione, et invicem conjunctione conficiuntur literae, ex quibus mirifica verba consistunt. Autore Francisco Georgio Veneto.

<cite>Note 550, p. 164</cite>

The satire — claiming that the book of Tobit is canonical because, as rabbis and esoteric theologians demonstrate, the letters of miraculous words can be derived from the variously twisted and combined hairs of Tobit's dog's tail — mocks both the kabbalistic letter-mysticism and Zorzi's exegetical method. Campanini refers to SIMPSON 1930, pp. 19–21 and 31, and CAMPANINI 2006.

## Agrippa and Contemporaries (notes 546–548)

Notes 546–548 situate Zorzi among contemporaries: Robert Fludd (AMMAN 1967; HUTIN 1971; GODWIN 1979), Pontus de Tyard (HUTIN 1966; SCHULZE 1967), and Heinrich Cornelius Agrippa von Nettesheim, with detailed references to Vittoria Perrone Compagni's editorial and scholarly work on Agrippa's De triplici ratione cognoscendi Deum and De incertitudine et vanitate scientiarum.

## Biblical Citation Block (notes 559–579)

The final sequence of notes (559–579) is a dense alphabetical-style index of scriptural passages cited at De Harmonia Mundi 3,8,20,2 and related sections. These include: 1 Timothy 6:16 (God dwelling in unapproachable light), Matthew 11:27 / Luke 10:22 (no one knows the Father except the Son), Proverbs 18:4, 1 Peter 1:25, Hebrews 1:3, Psalms 95:4, 144:3, 75:8, 7:10, 61:13, Job 24:12, John 14:6, 4 Esdras 7:135, Sirach 2:13 (with 2 Chronicles 30:9), the Exodus / Numbers / Nehemiah / Psalms cluster on divine mercy (Exodus 34:6; Numbers 14:18; Nehemiah 9:17; Psalms 86:15; 103:8; 145:8), Luke 18:7, Revelation 2:17, Isaiah 30:15, Lamentations 3:26, and Psalm 88:5. These citations anchor Zorzi's harmonistic reading of divine names and attributes to a broad scriptural base spanning Old and New Testaments and deuterocanonical texts.

## Significance for Zorzi's Project

These notes collectively demonstrate how Campanini's edition functions as a rigorous source-critical apparatus for a text whose kabbalistic learning was real but imprecisely transmitted. The corrections regarding Abulafia, the identification of Botarel, the unmasking of bibliographic phantoms, and the tracking of the French translator's error all illuminate how Zorzi's synthetic Christian-Kabbalist cosmology was constructed from, and subsequently distorted by, a complex chain of manuscript and print transmission stretching from medieval Sepharad to Venetian humanism and beyond.
